step.title: Step 2
step.actionDisplay: LEFT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> LEFT -> RESET

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 50 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions (first 10): LEFT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 52, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- Plan: First inspect the current frame to see where the player ended up, then probe the movement mechanics with a small test.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 51 actions; a strong score needs about 19 or fewer. You are 2.7x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 51 actions returned the board to a state already seen 51 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 51 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The player (charcoal 'c') is still at (16, 17) - the same starting position! The board didn't change at all. The 50 actions were executed but the player didn't move. This means my actions didn't actually move the player.
Wait, but the result said "board_changed": True. Let me check what actually changed. Maybe the magenta bar at the bottom changed (HUD/timer)?
Also, the game says "game_over": True but the player is still at the start. This is confusing.
Let me re-examine: the player is at (16, 17), the blue start is at boundary [[15, 15], [15, 17], [16, 16], [17, 17], [17, 15]] with 8 pixels. The player is 1 pixel at (16, 17).
The game is "over" but we can still take actions (valid_actions is non-empty). Maybe "game_over" here means something else - perhaps a timeout or the level failed.
But the key issue: the player didn't move! My 50 actions had no effect on the player's position. This means either:
1. The actions don't move the player as I assumed
2. The player can't move in those directions (blocked by walls)
3. The game mechanics are different from what I assumed
Wait, looking at the efficiency budget message: "your last 51 actions returned the board to a state already seen 51 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 51 actions." This confirms the board is back to the original state.
But the result said "board_changed": True. Maybe only the HUD changed, not the gameplay state.
Let me try a single action to see what happens. Maybe the player moves differently than I assumed.
Actually, wait. Let me re-read the problem. The game says "game_over" but we can still act. Maybe the game reset to the initial state? Or maybe the game_over was a false positive?
Let me try a simple probe: move RIGHT once and see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model revision: The player is STILL at (16, 17) - the starting position. Despite 50 actions being executed, the player didn't move at all. The board appears unchanged (except possibly HUD elements). The "game_over" state may indicate a timeout or failed attempt, but we can still act. Need to probe the actual movement mechanics.
Plan: Try a single RIGHT action to see if the player actually moves, and check what changes.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# First, let me check what the initial state looks like vs current
# The player is at (16, 17). Let me try moving RIGHT and see what happens.

# Check the current frame's magenta bar (possible timer)
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'M':
        print(f"Magenta bar: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Try a single RIGHT action
predict(expect_change=True, note='Test: moving RIGHT should move player from (16,17) to (16,18)')
action(['RIGHT'])

# Check where player is now
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'c':
        print(f"Player after RIGHT: boundary={n['boundary']}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

print(f"Step: {current_frame.step}")
print(f"Result: {last_action_result}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
